romanized: Yan mai gu wu yin liao (di tang)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general oat beverage class; no specific 'less sugar' variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX covers cereal-based beverages broadly; no specific oat cereal drink with reduced sugar sub-code exists. Closest defensible match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for oat cereal drink (less sugar); omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: mixed with hot or cold water

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (250 g) of prepared oat cereal drink, typically made by dissolving one sachet of powder in hot or warm water.

nutrient_source.title: Oat Cereal Drink (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Oat Cereal Drink (Less Sugar), cr_id D04010012. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; set to -1 with documentation.

gi_source.title: Oat-based cereal drink analogous estimate — University of Sydney GI Database; Wolever TM et al. (2004) oat-based beverage GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for this specific branded less-sugar oat cereal drink in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated based on the closest analogous food: oat-based cereal beverages with moderate sugar content, which cluster in the GI 50–60 range in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (25.65 g from 250 g serving) / 100 = 14.1, rounded to 14.2. The reduced-sugar formulation and low fibre content (0.82 g/100 g) support a low GI classification consistent with processed oat drinks, as GI 55 falls within the low GI range (<=55).

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar present (4.68 g/100 g); dietary fibre below 1 g/100 g; sodium moderate at 33 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Despite the 'less sugar' label, this oat cereal drink still contains added sugar and provides insufficient dietary fibre (<1 g/100 g) to confer meaningful cardiovascular benefit; individuals managing hypertension should prefer plain rolled oats or unsweetened oat milk to obtain beta-glucan fibre without added sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage classification; added sugar 4.68 g/100 g; negligible fibre (0.82 g/100 g) providing minimal glucose buffering; low GI (~55) but still an SSB
type2_diabetes.guidance: This product remains classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage under ADA guidelines even in its less-sugar formulation; the low fibre content provides negligible glucose buffering, and individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this drink in favour of plain oatmeal or unsweetened whole-grain options that deliver beta-glucan without added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar present; ultra-processed formulation; insufficient whole-grain fibre to confer cardioprotective beta-glucan benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While oats are associated with cardiovascular benefit through beta-glucan, this ultra-processed oat cereal drink delivers only 0.82 g dietary fibre per 100 g — far below the 3 g beta-glucan threshold recognised by health authorities for LDL reduction — and contains added sugar, making it a poor substitute for whole oat foods in a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar present; fibre below 1 g/100 g; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients identified
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This less-sugar oat drink provides no meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit: fibre remains below 1 g/100 g, added sugar is present, and no positive anti-inflammatory factors (omega-3, polyphenols, >3 g whole-food fibre, fermented food, cruciferous vegetables, or anti-inflammatory spices) are present; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose whole oat bran or rolled oats instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oat Cereal Drink (Less Sugar) is a commercially prepared, ultra-processed hot or cold beverage made from oats and mixed grains, formulated with a reduced quantity of added sugar compared to the standard variant. It is widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia as a convenient breakfast or snack drink, typically prepared by dissolving a sachet of powder in hot water. The drink has a mild, lightly sweet oat flavour with a smooth, slightly grainy texture. Per 100 g of prepared drink, it provides 57 kcal, 1.43 g protein, 1.17 g fat, 10.26 g carbohydrate, 4.68 g sugar, 0.82 g dietary fibre, 33 mg sodium, and 155 mg calcium (the latter reflecting calcium fortification). Despite its oat base, the fibre content is low at 0.82 g/100 g, well below the 3 g beta-glucan threshold associated with cardiovascular and glycaemic benefit. The 'less sugar' designation does not remove it from the sugar-sweetened beverage category under ADA or WHO guidelines. Clinically, this drink is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension due to its added sugar content and negligible fibre. It carries a low glycaemic index (~55) estimated from analogous oat-based cereal beverages. The product is vegetarian, vegan, halal, and dairy-free, but contains gluten from oats. It is fortified with calcium, making it a moderate source of that mineral. Whole oat alternatives such as plain rolled oats or unsweetened oat milk are strongly preferred for individuals seeking the genuine health benefits of oats.
common_user_queries: Is less sugar oat drink suitable for diabetics?
Can I drink oat cereal drink if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in oat cereal drink?
Is oat drink good for cholesterol?
What is the GI of oat cereal drink?
Is oat cereal drink suitable for weight loss?
Is less sugar oat drink still a sugar-sweetened beverage?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D04010012), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All core macros and key micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no exact entry exists for this product.
notes: iron_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB Singapore Foodo for this product; this is a confirmed zero per the approved source (HPB cr_id D04010012) and is retained as 0.0. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this product and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific formulation. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on oat-based cereal beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI entry exists for this specific less-sugar oat cereal drink. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 250 g serving size: GL = 55 × (10.26 × 2.5) / 100 = 55 × 25.65 / 100 = 14.1, reported as 14.2. The 'less sugar' formulation does not alter the sugar-sweetened beverage classification under ADA or WHO guidelines. Calcium value of 155 mg/100 g reflects product fortification as reported by HPB.
